critique.summary: The article presents Rob Pike's five rules of programming, emphasizing the importance of measuring performance before optimizing, favoring simple algorithms and data structures, and prioritizing data organization. It also connects these rules to well-known programming principles and quotes from other prominent computer scientists.
mainPoints: Do not guess where a program's bottlenecks are; measure first before optimizing.
Only optimize for speed when one part of the code dominates performance.
Avoid fancy algorithms for small data sizes as they have large constants and are often slower.
Simple algorithms and data structures are preferable because they are less buggy and easier to implement.
Choosing the right data structures is more important than complex algorithms; data organization is central to programming.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article accurately reflect Rob Pike's well-documented programming philosophy, which aligns with established principles in software engineering such as Tony Hoare's 'Premature optimization is the root of all evil' and Fred Brooks' emphasis on data structures in 'The Mythical Man-Month.' The article correctly attributes these ideas and summarizes them faithfully without distortion.
considerations: The article does not provide direct citations or links to original sources such as Rob Pike's talks or writings, which would strengthen credibility.
It does not discuss scenarios where advanced algorithms might be necessary despite small data sizes, such as real-time systems or specific performance constraints.
The article assumes familiarity with programming concepts and does not elaborate on the trade-offs involved in algorithm and data structure choices.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Rob Pike's programming rules, with particular focus on the often-misquoted phrase 'Premature optimization is the root of all evil,' its origins, and the importance of data structures over algorithms. Commenters share personal experiences and historical clarifications, emphasizing thoughtful design and the evolving role of AI in programming.

comments.author: anymouse123456
comments.text:
There are very few phrases in all of history that have done more damage to the project of software development than:

"Premature optimization is the root of all evil."

First, let's not besmirch the good name of Tony Hoare. The quote is from Donald Knuth, and the missing context is essential.

From his 1974 paper, "Structured Programming with go to Statements":

"Programmers waste enormous amounts of time thinking about, or worrying about, the speed of noncritical parts of their programs, and these attempts at efficiency actually have a strong negative impact when debugging and maintenance are considered. We should forget about small efficiencies, say about 97% of the time: premature optimization is the root of all evil. Yet we should not pass up our opportunities in that critical 3%."

He was talking about using GOTO statements in C. He was talking about making software much harder to reason about in the name of micro-optimizations. He assumed (incorrectly) that we would respect the machines our software runs on.

Multiple generations of programmers have now been raised to believe that brutally inefficient, bloated, and slow software is just fine. There is no limit to the amount of boilerplate and indirection a computer can be forced to execute. There is no ceiling to the crystalline abstractions emerging from these geniuses. There is no amount of time too long for a JVM to spend starting.

I worked at Google many years ago. I have lived the absolute nightmares that evolve from the willful misunderstanding of this quote.

No thank you. Never again.

I have committed these sins more than any other, and I'm mad as hell about it.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-informed correction about the quote's origin and context, highlighting the nuanced meaning often lost in popular usage.

comments.author: DaleBiagio
comments.text:
The attribution to Hoare is a common error — "Premature optimization is the root of all evil" first appeared in Knuth's 1974 paper "Structured Programming with go to Statements."

Knuth later attributed it to Hoare, but Hoare said he had no recollection of it and suggested it might have been Dijkstra.

Rule 5 aged the best. "Data dominates" is the lesson every senior engineer eventually learns the hard way.
comments.analysis: This comment adds historical detail and clarifies the common misattribution, reinforcing the importance of accurate sourcing in programming lore.

comments.author: ta20211004_1
comments.text:
Can't agree more on 5. I've repeatedly found that any really tricky programming problem is (eventually) solved by iterative refinement of the data structures (and the APIs they expose / are associated with). When you get it right the control flow of a program becomes straightforward to reason about.

To address our favorite topic: while I use LLMs to assist on coding tasks a lot, I think they're very weak at this. Claude is much more likely to suggest or expand complex control flow logic on small data types than it is to recognize and implement an opportunity to encapsulate ideas in composable chunks. And I don't buy the idea that this doesn't matter since most code will be produced and consumed by LLMs. The LLMs of today are much more effective on code bases that have already been thoughtfully designed. So are humans. Why would that change?
comments.analysis: The comment offers practical insight into the importance of data structures in solving complex problems and critically assesses the current capabilities of LLMs in code design.

comments.author: kleiba
comments.text: I believe the "premature evil" quote is by Knuth, not Hoare?!
comments.analysis: A brief but accurate correction regarding the quote's attribution, aligning with more detailed comments on the topic.

comments.author: keyle
comments.text:
Rule 5 is definitely king. Code acts on data, if the data is crap, you're already lost.

edit: s/data/data structure/
comments.analysis: Concise and valid emphasis on the primacy of data quality and structure in programming success.

comments.author: embedding-shape
comments.text:
"Epigrams in Programming" by Alan J. Perlis has a lot more, if you like short snippets of wisdom :) https://www.cs.yale.edu/homes/perlis-alan/quotes.html

> Rule 5. Data dominates. If you've chosen the right data structures and organized things well, the algorithms will almost always be self-evident. Data structures, not algorithms, are central to programming.

Always preferred Perlis' version, that might be slightly over-used in functional programming to justify all kinds of hijinks, but with some nuance works out really well in practice:

> 9. It is better to have 100 functions operate on one data structure than 10 functions on 10 data structures.
comments.analysis: Provides additional context and references related to programming wisdom, enriching the discussion around data and algorithms.

comments.author: CharlieDigital
comments.text:
I feel like 1 and 2 are only applicable in cases of novelty.

The thing is, if you build enough of the same kinds of systems in the same kinds of domains, you can kinda tell where you should optimize ahead of time.

Most of us tend to build the same kinds of systems and usually spend a career or a good chunk of our careers in a given domain. I feel like you can't really be considered a staff/principal if you can't already tell ahead of time where the perf bottleneck will be just on experience and intuition.
comments.analysis: Offers a nuanced perspective on optimization, suggesting experience can guide early optimization decisions in familiar domains.

comments.author: piranha
comments.text:
> Rule 5 is often shortened to "write stupid code that uses smart objects".

This is probably the worst use of the word "shortened" ever, and it should be more like "mutilated"?
comments.analysis: Expresses frustration over a misinterpretation of Rule 5, highlighting the importance of preserving original meaning.

comments.author: Devasta
comments.text:
> "Premature optimization is the root of all evil."

This Axiom has caused far and away more damage to software development than the premature optimization ever will.
comments.analysis: Strongly worded but valid critique on the negative impact of misapplying the premature optimization advice.

comments.author: igtztorrero
comments.text:
Rule 4, I have always practiced and demanded of junior programmers, to make algorithms and structures that are simple to understand, for our main user: the one who will modify this code in the future.

I believe that's why Golang is a very simple but powerful language.
comments.analysis: Highlights the importance of code simplicity and maintainability, reinforcing a key principle in software engineering.
